title: Introducing new sheep to an existing flock
subtitle: Six weeks of quarantine, movement rules, and a step-by-step integration plan.
excerpt: Buying new sheep is one of the most common ways disease enters a smallholding. A six-week quarantine period, a thorough health check on arrival, and a staged pasture introduction protect both the incoming animals and your existing flock. This tutorial covers the legal movement requirements and the practical steps from unloading to full integration.
sourceNotes:
DEFRA, Sheep and Goat Keepers: How to Keep Records and Report Movements. https://www.gov.uk
AHDB Beef and Lamb, Better Returns Programme: Biosecurity on Sheep Farms (2013). https://ahdb.org.uk
NADAIS Biosecurity for Sheep. https://www.nadis.org.uk

glossaryTerms.definition: A period of isolation for newly arrived animals, kept separate from the existing flock on different pasture or in a dedicated pen, to allow monitoring for disease and to carry out health treatments before contact with resident animals. UK sheep health guidance recommends a minimum of four weeks; six weeks is the standard recommended by most biosecurity advisors.

A legal restriction under the Sheep and Goats (Records, Identification and Movement) Order 2009 that prevents any sheep or goat from leaving a premises for six days after an animal arrives. The standstill begins at midnight on the day of arrival and applies to the whole holding, not just the new animals.

A statutory record that every keeper of sheep and goats in England must maintain. It records each animal's ear tag number, date of birth (or approximate age), breed, sex, date of arrival, source holding CPH number, and date of departure with destination. Inspected by Animal and Plant Health Agency (APHA) officers on farm visits.

content.text: The day new animals arrive is day one of a six-week process, not a one-off event. Bringing sheep onto a smallholding without a quarantine plan risks introducing scab, footrot, Johnes disease, resistant worms, or caseous lymphadenitis to a previously clean flock. None of those are quick to clear.
Legal requirements on the day of arrival
A
movement standstill
automatically begins the moment new sheep arrive. No sheep or goat may leave the holding for six days. The movement must be recorded in your
holding register

within 36 hours. You must also report the movement to the Animal Reporting and Movement Service (ARAMS) or your local APHA office if ARAMS is unavailable. Keep the movement document (AML2 or equivalent) from the previous keeper for at least three years.
Setting up a quarantine area
New sheep must be kept physically separate from your existing flock for the full
quarantine period

. Separate means different pasture with a sheep-proof fence between them, or different pens in the same building with no shared air space if housing is necessary. Do not use shared water troughs, feeding equipment, or handling equipment between the two groups without disinfecting between uses. Change footwear or use a footbath when moving between quarantine and home flock areas.
Health checks on arrival

Carry out a full health check before the animals leave the trailer. Weigh each animal if possible: an accurate weight is needed for correct dosing of any treatments. Check body condition score, foot health, eye clarity, and fleece condition. Look for any signs of skin lesions, limping, or nasal discharge.
Worm on arrival with a product from a different class than the previous keeper used, ideally based on a faecal egg count result.
Foot-trim and footbathe all animals.
Check vaccination records. Give a clostridial booster if the history is unknown or the booster is overdue.
Tag any untagged animals with your flock mark before they leave the trailer.
The six-week quarantine period

Check the quarantine group at least once a day. Record any treatments in the holding register. At week three, carry out a faecal egg count to assess the worm burden; treat if the count is above the threshold recommended by your vet or SCOPS guidelines. Look for signs of scab (intense rubbing against fences, bare patches in the fleece) in the first two weeks; scab mites have a two-week life cycle, so signs may not appear for 14 days after contact.

attrs.title: When to call your vet
attrs.body: Sheep scab (Psoroptes ovis) is a notifiable disease. If you suspect it, call your vet before treating. Maedi Visna and Caseous Lymphadenitis (CLA) are both slow-developing conditions that will not show up in a six-week quarantine. Ask the vendor directly about flock history for both before you buy.

content.text: Gradual integration

After six weeks with no health concerns, introduce the new animals to the existing flock on pasture rather than in a pen. Putting both groups into a confined space at once increases fighting and stress. Put them in adjacent fields separated by a fence for two or three days so they can smell and see each other before the fence comes down. Monitor for bullying in the first week. Older ewes will establish a pecking order; some chasing is normal. Separate any animal that is being prevented from reaching feed or water.
